title: It transformed Australian sport. Now the WBBL is a boardroom afterthought
text:
Advertisement

SportCricketBig Bash Opinion It transformed Australian sport. Now the WBBL is a boardroom afterthought

Sarah Styles June 17, 2026 — 2:30pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The loudest voices in Australian cricket are currently shaping the debate on the future of the Big Bash. Phenomenal windfalls seen around the Indian Premier League and the Hundred in England, combined with increased global jostling for Twenty20 talent and a disrupted market tightening sums available via broadcast rights, have decision makers narrowing in on not whether privatisation is the right path, but rather what form that path should take. Australian star Ellyse Perry. Dominic Lorrimer I’ll leave it to others to discuss the merits, and pitfalls, of privatisation, with that topic extensively debated. Instead, I’m interested in the topic that is barely rating a mention at all.

Advertisement

Related Article Exclusive

Australian cricket They’re alive: Melbourne Stars revived after two-week merger saga Where is the Women’s Big Bash League in all of this? Reading the coverage and listening to interviews with the sport’s leaders, you could be forgiven for forgetting one of the world’s premier women’s cricket competitions even exists. Occasionally, the WBBL slips in as a throwaway comment at the back end; something that comes attached to the asset being sold, like the steak knives in the old Demtel ads, as opposed to being an asset in its own right. And frankly, I could do without the sense of deja vu. It wasn’t always this way.

Advertisement

Significant effort and investment went into rewiring cricket to respect and realise the value of women’s cricket. The creation of the WBBL in 2015 marked a profound shift in how Australian cricket viewed the opportunity that is women and girls in sport. For the first time, women’s cricket was not being reduced to a cost centre or a participation program. It was recognised as a product capable of attracting fans, sponsors, broadcasters and investment. Australian star Annabel Sutherland on the attack for the Melbourne Stars in the WBBL. Getty Images That belief helped transform not only Australian cricket, but Australian sport. AFLW, NRLW and Super W were just some of the developments that followed. It contributed to unprecedented participation growth; within just a few years, there was a 2000 per cent increase in all-girls teams played out in community clubs across the country.

Advertisement

It built household names. It helped retain key commercial partners. It culminated in the largest women’s cricket match in history, and the largest women’s sporting event ever held in Australia, with a sold-out MCG for the Women’s T20 World Cup final in 2020. It demonstrated what was possible when women’s sport was backed with ambition and resources. Matildas captain Sam Kerr (left) is a global icon. Getty Images Everything that’s been said about the Matildas over the past three years was said about women’s cricket first, just as the world slid into the COVID-19 pandemic. It is true that the men’s Big Bash League remains the overwhelming driver of broadcast and sponsorship revenue. By extension, a BBL team will be more valuable today than a WBBL team.

Advertisement

But for WBBL to not even feature in the public debate? That’s a mistake. Investors worldwide are increasingly recognising women’s teams as one of the industry’s most significant growth opportunities, and an asset class on the rise. Loading It’s barely three years since more than $800 million was paid for five licences to launch the Women’s Premier League. Around the world, funds worth more than $2 billion have been launched solely to invest in women’s sport properties. In the US, National Women’s Soccer League expansion fees have grown 200-fold since 2020, while the WNBA’s Golden State Valkyries recently became the world’s first billion-dollar women’s sport team. Not a single dollar from that $2 billion is currently directed at Australia.

Advertisement

Related Article Australian cricket ‘You’ve let the fox into the henhouse’: Inside the fight for cricket’s future So again, why aren’t we talking about the WBBL, if the driver for privatisation is purely economic? Regardless of whether internal conversations within cricket’s boardrooms differ, at the very least, the cricketing public is once again being told that women’s cricket isn’t part of the main game. And you cannot build excitement, let alone investment, in an asset you rarely bother to discuss. Sarah Styles is the former Director of the Office for Women in Sport and Recreation, a non-executive director of the Victorian Institute of Sport, former investment banker and served as Cricket Australia’s inaugural head of female engagement from 2014-20. Barkly’s mineralisation is held in a sedimentary-hosted system of loose sands, a style that is uncommon in Australia but offers distinct advantages. The deposit shows strong consistency over wide areas and being essentially mobile cover, requires minimal crushing and screening, pointing to a potentially low-cost mining and processing operation. Barkly is wasting little time getting on top of its chemistry, with the first grant supporting a program to de-risk the project’s metallurgical flowsheet. The work has built on historical small-scale testing, which produced some impressive early results, including a 74 per cent extraction of high-value magnet rare earths. Adding another string to its bow, the project hosts a 200-million-tonne vanadium resource grading 0.12 per cent vanadium oxide, which overlies the rare earths zone, offering a handy by-product option should Barkly choose to pursue it.

Advertisement

As the drill rods get set to spin next month, management will be looking to test the scale of its massive exploration target at Barkly, which ranges from 200 million to a whopping one billion tonnes of resource grading between 1600ppm and 1900ppm TREO. With cash in the bank from its recent IPO, a solid starter resource, a monster exploration target and now two separate government grants in its back pocket, Barkly appears to be stepping on the gas early. Whilst the NT Government’s funding might only be a small piece of the puzzle, a double cash hit appears a solid vote of confidence in the project’s potential. With the met work ticking along nicely, all eyes now move to what the “truth-telling” drill bit might uncover. Is your ASX-listed company doing something interesting? title: Former Top Gear host Jeremy Clarkson reveals cancer diagnosis
text:
Advertisement

CultureCelebrityCancer Former Top Gear host Jeremy Clarkson reveals cancer diagnosis Kayla Olaya June 17, 2026 — 1:33pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Jeremy Clarkson, one of the long-time hosts of the popular BBC show Top Gear, has revealed he has been diagnosed with an “aggressive” form of cancer. The 66-year-old television presenter shared the news in the latest episode of his Amazon series Clarkson’s Farm, while sitting with farmworkers Kaleb Cooper and Charlie Ireland talking about the timing of the property’s next harvests. Jeremy Clarkson has revealed he is undergoing surgery for cancer. Getty Images “I’ve got cancer,” Clarkson says suddenly during the conversation in episode seven of the series’ fifth season. Cooper, stunned, replies: “No, you haven’t. Where?” “Where it is is of no concern to anybody. I’ve known since May,” Clarkson replies, as Cooper wipes away tears.

Advertisement

“I had a medical, you remember, back in May? I disappeared off the other week and I had a biopsy done and it is cancer, and it’s aggressive, but it’s really early, so the treatment will be, you know. “I’m praying we could get the harvest done and then I could go and get some treatment, but it’s going to be slap bang in the middle.” Cooper quickly assures Clarkson to forget about the harvest. “Look after yourself,” he says. Clarkson then reveals that he is scheduled to have surgery. “It’s going to be in about two weeks, maybe three,” he says. “I’ll have to go and have an operation … then [I’ll be] slightly out of action for a little while.”

Advertisement

Clarkson revealed during a later conversation that he had undergone a procedure to have part of his prostate removed following the diagnosis. “The prostate, 10 per cent of it’s dead,” he said. “The 10 per cent where the cancer is.” At the end of the season finale, Clarkson addresses viewers from the hospital bed. “We started season five with me in a hospital bed, and here we are at the end of season five, and I’m back in a hospital bed,” he says. “Some of the treatments have gone a bit awry, let’s say. So I am going to be here for a little while … I’m nil by mouth, yeah, I don’t know what’s going to happen … What I wanted to say was, if this is all successful, I will see you in season 6, and if it isn’t, I won’t.

Advertisement

“Take care, everyone.” More to come

title: Huge win for coal miner after 'earthquake' blast
text:
Despite an open-cut mine explosion landing two workers in hospital, the miner has been cleared of criminal charges.

Maules Creek Coal and its parent company Whitehaven Coal had four convictions successfully overturned in the NSW Court of Criminal Appeal on Wednesday after the court found explosives were handled appropriately.

The miner was found guilty of breaching environmental protection laws in 2024 after an August 2020 blast at the Maules Creek open-cut coal mine in Boggabri in northern NSW.

It had used nearly 900,000kg of explosives in its bid to extract coal, which was about 50,000kg more than its modelling had called for.

The resulting explosion exceeded noise limits and left two workers at a different coal mine more than a kilometre away needing hospital treatment.

The court found Maules Creek Mine not proven to have acted incompetently in setting up the blast. (Dean Lewins/AAP PHOTOS)

Maules Creek Coal was ordered to pay a total fine of $200,000 and publish a notice outlining the judgment and penalties.

But on Wednesday the appeal judges found it was not clear that the overloading of the explosives caused the excessive noise and that the company was not proven to have acted incompetently in setting up the blast.

A miner living 12km from the Maules Creek Mine, near Boggabri, said the "terrifying" explosion was bigger than anything she had experienced in her 17-year career.

Another local told the original NSW Land and Environment Court hearing that she had wondered if it was an earthquake and her lounge chair rocked during the blast on August 20, 2020.

Two workers at the adjacent Boggabri coal mine were taken to hospital for checks after the incident, with one saying the "almighty pop" left him with tinnitus and severe sleep problems in the months afterwards.

But the trio of appeal judges unanimously found the Land and Environment Court could not find the company failed to meet its obligations under its environmental protection licence.

"Subsequent modelling ... raised reasonable doubt as to the allegation that the materials were not handled in a proper and efficient manner," Justices Julie Ward, Mark Leeming, and Desmond Fagan wrote.

They quashed Maules Creek Coal's convictions and instead recorded a verdict of acquittal on four charges.

A final determination on the costs stemming from the original trial will be heard in the Land and Environment Court at a later date.

title: Get Up! hijacks Pauline Hanson’s NPC address
text:
Pauline Hanson has blasted the mainstream media in an address to the National Press Club, saying it doesn’t have the right to “delegitimise” her One Nation party, as left wing campaigners disrupted the event.

The One Nation leader on Wednesday gave her first address to the forum, where she gave an often controversial speech, including declaring Australia “cannot be a multicultural society”.

Advocacy group GetUp has claimed responsibility for a stunt in which a protest poster was unfurled behind Senator Hanson as she delivered her address.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

In a statement, GetUp chief executive Paul Ferris said the stunt aimed to highlight Senator Hanson’s voting record.

“Pauline Hanson has built her entire brand on being for the battlers. But her record tells a different story,” he said.

Pauline Hanson has delivered her first ever speech to the National Press Club on Wednesday. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

Protesters managed to use a banned gatecrash Pauline Hanson's National Press Club address. Pic: NewsWire Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

“One Nation has consistently opposed wage rises, affordable childcare, increases to the aged pension, and housing affordability measures.

“We thought the occasion deserved some honesty. So we provided it.”

An organiser said there would not be “stunts” at the National Press Club. Senator Hanson continued with her address.

She proceeded to speak emotionally, on the verge of tears, of the plight of low-income Australians, including those that had sent their children to school hungry.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

But later took aim at journalists.

“It’s your job to scrutinise my party, its people and its policies,” she told an audience made up largely of members of the federal parliament press gallery.

Senator Hanson said she welcomed scrutiny of One Nation, its people and its policies but said that didn’t give the media the “licence to pile on”.

“Unlike the Prime Minister, I welcome it. I’m an elected representative and I should be scrutinised,” she said.

“That doesn’t give you the licence to pile on. It doesn’t give you the licence to delegitimise my party.

“It doesn’t give you the licence to say preferencing One Nation is immoral.”

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

She said the media had no right “to repeat the lie that we are a racist Party – because that is untrue.”

“The Australian people can make up their own mind. And they are,” she said.

“Rest assured, there will be big changes if One Nation is given the chance.

“The SBS will be gone. There’s no need for it anymore; the internet has overtaken the need for it.

“The ABC will still exist, but in a very different form.”

Pauline Hanson’s speech covered the main issues she was promoting. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

A One Nation government would fund the national broadcaster only in regional areas, while in cities, it would be a “subscription service”.

She accused the ABC of having an “obvious” bias against One Nation.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“Remember this: I don’t answer to the media. I answer to the Australian people,” she said.

“Australians’ trust in the media, the government and public institutions is at an all-time low.

“To you, the media, may I offer some advice – you have to earn the trust of the Australian people.”

Protesters gatecrash Hanson’s NPC address

Early into the speech, a poster stating Senator Hanson “opposed pay a rise for workers … while I took a $100,000 pay rise for myself” appeared from the rear screen during her address, with the One Nation leader forced to briefly pause as staff removed the prop.

Pauline Hanson continued to give her address despite the disruption. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

“How can we, as members of Parliament who are supposed to represent the people of this nation, allow that to happen? It is disgraceful,” she said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

More than 30 protesters had demonstrated outside National Press Club in Canberra as Senator Hanson arrived for the address.

The group had accused the Queensland senator of “using ordinary people as cover for her vile racism”, under the auspice of at least 10 police officers.

“When she first declared that Australia was in danger of being swamped by Asians, she was a racist,” one speaker said.

“She was a racist when she warned that Muslim women are national security threat.

“She is this country’s most prominent, most serious, most popular racist, and that is why we have to fight against Hanson.”

Demonstrators have protested the One Nation leader’s stance on migration and energy. Picture: Nathan Schmidt / NewsWire

‘Trashy journalist’

Senator Hanson has lashed out at a journalist who asked whether the One Nation leader used nepotism to secure her daughter Lee a taxpayer-funded job as a senior adviser to a NSW One Nation politician.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Sarah Martin, a journalist for the Guardian, asked if the Queensland politician had any role in “appointing” Lee to the office of NSW parliamentarian Sean Bell.

“Honestly, you never give up. I’ve never seen a person that’s such a trashy journalist, you know, and what you put out all the time,” Senator Hanson said.

“You’ve got this obsession with constantly trying to pull down myself, my party, or Mrs Rinehart.”

Senator Hanson fired off at a journalist who asked about her daughter Lee (pictured) who was employed in a One Nation politician’s office. Picture: NewsWire / David Crosling

She threatened Ms Martin not to “come near” her for an interview in the future, accusing her of “constantly bashing One Nation”.

You don’t research properly … you will put out lies about me, well, I’ve had enough of that,” she said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Senator Hanson denied getting her daughter the job, claiming it was “on her own merits by someone who actually wanted to employ her”.

She later said she “didn’t believe in nepotism”.

Hanson pledges to sack human rights, sex discrimination chiefs

Senator Hanson described transgender Australians as an “insurgency” and pledged to sack officials in support of what she described as “transgender ideology”.

She singled out Sex Discrimination Commissioner, Dr Anna Cody and Human Rights Commission President Hugh de Kretser who she said she would “sack” over their views on transgender issues.

But she said she wasn’t “opposed” to transgender people.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“I am simply saying that a transgender woman should not be allowed into woman’s sport or into a woman’s changing room. And likewise, a transgender man,” she said.

Farrer a “watershed moment” for One Nation

The hotly-contested Farrer by-election, in which One Nation clinched the formerly blue-ribbon seat, was a “watershed moment for the populist party, Senator Hanson said.

“We had breached that final barrier – a One Nation candidate with strong credentials in David Farley, won us a seat in the House of Representatives for the first time,” she said.

“And not just any seat, but a seat previously held by former representatives of the Liberals and the Nationals. “

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

She referenced GetUp’s $600,000 campaign to target One Nation, saying all it did was drive “more votes our way”.

The campaign had centred on the populist leader’s affinity for Donald Trump and links her to the rising costs that rural voters are most vulnerable to, including fuel and fertiliser.

Government lying about CGT reform: Hanson

Senator Hanson doubled down on her accusation the government was “not telling the truth” on its controversial tax reforms – touted to tackle intergenerational inequity in the housing market – and accused Anthony Albanese and Jim Chalmers of misleading young Australians.

“Punishing ambitious and aspirational young people who are trying to build a future, to build wealth, to build independence and to invest in Australia, Albanese and Chalmers are heading in the wrong direction,” she said.

Pauline Hanson has taken aim at the Albanese government’s CGT policy. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

Pauline Hanson and her primary adviser James Ashby before she delivered her speech at the National Press Club in Canberra. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

She invoked the Coalition’s attack line of spruiking Peter Costello’s debt-free declaration in April 2006.

“It is 20 years since Peter Costello made that declaration. In that time the debt has climbed from zero to almost $1 trillion,” she said.

Senator Hanson later took a personal swipe at Mr Chalmers – whose PhD dissertation focused on Paul Keating – saying he “hasn’t learnt much”.

“Paul Keating reduced spending to 22.9 per cent of GDP,” she said.

Senator Hanson was adamant she wasn’t afraid of causing offence with her actions or words, saying every attempt had “been made for years to silence me”.

“In fact, there are always some people, usually a lot of people, who get offended by what I say or do, even offended by my very existence,” she said.

“I don’t fear it. I embrace it. But many people aren’t like me. In Australia today, in our democracy, the real tragedy is people are frightened of what will happen to them if they just speak up.”

She claimed civil debate had been “paralysed” and accused the media of being “complicit in amplifying this fear”.

Hanson opposes multiculturalism

Speaking to a packed-out crowd at the press club, including her chief of staff James Ashby, as well as colleagues Barnaby Joyce, Malcolm Roberts, Sean Bell and Tyrone Whitten, Senator Hanson declared Australia “cannot be a multicultural society”.

“We are a multiracial society, but we must be monocultural. Australians must live under the one cultural umbrella,” she said.

Pauline Hanson said Western culture was “under siege”. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

Pauline Hanson was joined by One Nation colleagues at her address at the National Press Club of Australia in Canberra. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

Barnaby Joyce defected to One Nation from the Nationals at the end of last year. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

She described multiculturalism as a “failed policy” and claimed opposing it was “not racist”.

“Surely opposing that is not racist, it’s common sense,” she said.

ON to include nuclear in energy policy

She later confirmed One Nation would wind back Labor’s renewable energy policy, claiming it had fuelled the affordability crisis, but flagged the party supported nuclear energy in its mix.

“One Nation is saying, put everything into the energy mix,” she said.

“Let everyone share the wealth that is under our feet.

“We are one of the richest resource nations in the world.

“I am saying get ideology out of the way.”

Protesters brand One Nation leader ‘dangerous’

Among the protesters outside, one speaker branded Senator Hanson “the most dangerous politician in Australia as she drives a racist far right body into the mainstream.”

Speakers also criticised the right-wing politician’s opposition to abortion rights and to clean energy policies.

Protesters said One Nation harboured “homophobes and white supremacists” and proceeded to chant “Pauline Hanson go to hell”.

One speaker said the protest was the first against Senator Hanson in Canberra “in decades” and vowed to continue the effort.

The group later led a march around the corner, past the National Press Club, chanting “Pauline Hanson has got to go” and “we will fight, we will win, put the racists in the bins”.

“Pauline Hanson go to hell, take your racists there too,” they shouted.

The march proceeded along Bourke St and onto Macquarie St, with two uniformed officers following the group, despite walking into traffic.
